narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the resilience it takes to pursue your dreams in a field that needs more voices like yours. Your profile includes an undergraduate GPA of 2.90, an LSAT score of 23, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 3, a middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated your probability of failing the bar at 44%—a risk level that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger predicted odds of passing on the first try. The most influential factor pulling in a positive direction—toward predicting you'd pass—was your undergraduate GPA, which, though below the typical 3.21 average, still carried significant weight in showing your academic potential. However, your LSAT score had the strongest opposing pull, pushing the prediction toward failure because it was well below the dataset average of 36.70, standing out as a major concern compared to most applicants. Your Year 3 decile, also below the average of 5.53, contributed somewhat toward expecting success but not enough to offset other issues. Similarly, your middle family income and full-time enrollment plans nudged things slightly toward passing, aligning with common profiles of successful students, though neither was a dominant force. Your Year 1 decile, like your Year 3, was lower than the typical 5.71 average and added a small push toward the failure prediction. Overall, while some aspects of your background mirror those of students who succeed, your LSAT score was the standout hurdle when compared to the broader group, tipping the model's decision against admission. This isn't a reflection of your worth or potential—many students overcome similar starts through targeted preparation. Next, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, strengthening your GPA with any post-undergrad coursework, and seeking mentorship programs tailored for Black women in law to build a standout reapplication. You're capable of turning this around; keep pushing forward with that fierce determination.
